name: author
context: Author OpenAPI contracts with stable operation IDs, reusable components, examples, errors, and security.
info: OpenAPI authoring is the skill of creating and maintaining a contract that humans, agents, validators, mocks, tests, and code generators can all rely on. The execution surface is a YAML or JSON document, usually `openapi.yaml`, plus scripts that scaffold a baseline document and validation tools that keep it correct. Agents use this node to design new HTTP APIs, document existing ones, normalize framework-generated specs, add examples, define common error envelopes, describe authentication, and make generated SDK output stable by choosing durable operation IDs and schemas.
triggers: add examples to the api spec
make generated client method names stable
body:
# OpenAPI Authoring

Authoring turns an HTTP API design into a durable contract. Write the contract as if downstream tools will be strict: generated SDK names come from `operationId`, test inputs come from schemas, mock responses come from examples and schema constraints, and documentation renders exactly what the description says.

## Scaffold

```bash
nodes/openapi/scripts/scaffold-openapi.mjs --out openapi.yaml --title Billing --version 1.0.0 --server http://localhost:3000
```

Then validate immediately:

```bash
nodes/openapi/scripts/validate-openapi.sh openapi.yaml
```

## Authoring Checklist

1. Set `openapi`, `info.title`, `info.version`, and `servers`.
2. Add paths with concrete HTTP methods and stable `operationId` values.
3. Define every path, query, header, and cookie parameter with `name`, `in`, `required`, and `schema`.
4. Define request bodies for methods that accept payloads.
5. Define responses for success, validation errors, auth errors, not found, rate limits, and default failures.
6. Move shared schemas, responses, parameters, request bodies, examples, and security schemes into `components`.
7. Add examples that match schemas.
8. Run [[openapi/validate]] and fix warnings before using [[openapi/generate]], [[openapi/mock]], or [[openapi/contract-test]].

## Stable Operation IDs

Use names that describe the API action, not framework internals:

```yaml
paths:
  /customers/{customerId}/invoices:
    get:
      operationId: listCustomerInvoices
    post:
      operationId: createCustomerInvoice
```

Bad operation IDs include `CustomerController_index`, `get`, `apiV1CustomersInvoicesGet`, and names that change when routes move between files.

## Reusable Components

```yaml
components:
  parameters:
    CustomerId:
      name: customerId
      in: path
      required: true
      schema:
        type: string
  responses:
    NotFound:
      description: Resource not found
      content:
        application/json:
          schema:
            $ref: '#/components/schemas/Error'
  schemas:
    Error:
      type: object
      required: [code, message]
      properties:
        code: { type: string }
        message: { type: string }
        requestId: { type: string }
```

## Security

Define auth once, then apply globally or per operation:

```yaml
components:
  securitySchemes:
    bearerAuth:
      type: http
      scheme: bearer
      bearerFormat: JWT
security:
  - bearerAuth: []
```

Use `security: []` on a specific operation only when it is intentionally public.

sections.title: Authoring workflow
sections.body: Start from a scaffold or existing service behavior. Define the public contract first: resources, methods, request bodies, responses, pagination, auth, and errors. Keep operation IDs stable. Reuse components. Add examples. Validate after each meaningful edit with [[openapi/validate]]. Generate or mock only after validation is clean.

sections.title: Stable operation IDs
sections.body: `operationId` should be unique, stable, language-friendly, and action-oriented. Good examples: `listInvoices`, `createPayment`, `getCustomer`, `cancelSubscription`. Generated SDKs use these names for methods, so changing them is a client-facing breaking change even when URL paths stay the same.

sections.title: Components-first reuse
sections.body: Move repeated schemas, responses, parameters, request bodies, headers, examples, and security schemes into `components`. This reduces drift and lets agents update common API behavior once. Error envelopes, pagination parameters, IDs, auth schemes, and standard rate-limit responses should almost always be components.

sections.title: Examples
sections.body: Examples are executable documentation: mock servers use them, docs render them, and agents inspect them to infer intended behavior. Keep examples small, realistic, non-secret, and schema-valid. Prefer named examples for important variants such as success, validation failure, empty page, and unauthorized.

sections.title: Error responses
sections.body: Define one reusable `Error` schema and reusable responses for common status codes. At minimum describe 400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 422, 429, 500, and `default` when relevant. Generated clients and contract tests are more useful when failure shapes are explicit.

links.context: Object model and field semantics needed to write valid OpenAPI
Validate and bundle the authored contract
Use examples and schemas to run a mock server
Generate clients and server stubs from stable operations
Zod schemas can be a TypeScript source for OpenAPI schemas

related.note: Authoring depends on understanding OpenAPI objects and schema semantics
Every authored contract should be linted and bundled before use
Generated code quality depends heavily on authoring discipline

scripts.name: Scaffold OpenAPI Contract
scripts.description: Create a minimal OpenAPI YAML or JSON document with health endpoint, schemas, examples, and reusable error response

out.description: Output path such as openapi.yaml or docs/openapi.json

depends.reason: Authoring must follow the OpenAPI object model
The scaffold script runs on Node.js

frontmatter.audience: API designers, backend engineers, AI agents
